evidence: manage_symbols() in files.py handles GET/POST/DELETE for the symbols directory — 'manage' reveals nothing about the multi-method dispatch inside
runner() in multivol.py is the top-level orchestration entry point; the name gives no hint it sets up directories, checks Docker images, runs parallel plugin pools, and invokes strings extraction
getCommands() in multi_volatility2.py uses camelCase while every other function in the codebase uses snake_case (get_file_hash, calculate_sha256, list_images, etc.)
dump_dir parameter in execute_command_volatility2 is described by an inline comment as 'actually the full file path from main.py' — the name actively contradicts its content

The 'hash' key in the evidence response dict from list_evidences() is assigned source_dump (a filename string like 'memorydump.raw'), not a hash value; same field is set to item (also a filename) in the second pass
process_recover_fs() in utils.py uses the generic 'process' verb; the function reads a volatility JSON output, restructures it into a tree, and overwrites the file — closer to build_recover_fs_tree()

show_commands parameter in execute_command_volatility2 controls debug print output; the equivalent flag is called debug throughout the rest of the codebase (--debug CLI arg, getattr(arguments, 'debug', False) call site)

strengths: Most route handlers follow a precise verb-noun pattern: upload_file, list_evidences, delete_evidence, download_evidence — callers can predict what each does without reading the body
calculate_sha256 / get_file_hash cleanly distinguish pure computation from cached retrieval; the naming distinction documents the caching contract
Background-thread helpers are consistently prefixed: background_dump_task, background_scan, background_single_run — the naming convention makes concurrency boundaries visible
cleanup_timeouts, ingest_results_to_db, resolve_host_path are highly specific and accurately describe what they do
Module-level constants (STORAGE_DIR, BASE_DIR, UPLOAD_FOLDER, SIDECAR_BASE_PORT) are all uppercase and unambiguous
naming_quality.issue_character: Naming issues are scattered rather than systemic — a few generic verbs (manage, runner, process), one convention outlier (camelCase getCommands), and two concrete name/behavior mismatches (dump_dir parameter, hash API field) that would trip developers reading or consuming this code.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase is mostly well-named: the majority of functions communicate intent at a glance and several naming patterns are applied consistently. However, a developer will encounter enough friction — a generic route handler that hides three HTTP methods, a parameter whose name contradicts its content (acknowledged by an inline comment), and an API field called 'hash' that returns a filename — to occasionally need to read implementations rather than trust names. This places the codebase in the 70-75 range: mixed, with real but non-pervasive issues rather than systemic unreliability.

issues.summary: manage_symbols() hides three-way HTTP dispatch behind an opaque 'manage' verb
evidence: def manage_symbols() at line 43 handles GET (list), POST (upload), and DELETE (remove) in a single function; 'manage' does not tell a reader which operations are supported or how to use the endpoint
issues.suggestion: Split into three focused route handlers: list_symbols(), upload_symbol(), delete_symbol() — or at minimum rename to handle_symbols_request() and add a brief docstring enumerating the three methods and their parameters

issues.summary: runner() in multivol.py is a generic name for the top-level analysis orchestration function
evidence: def runner(arguments) at line 20 sets up directories, validates arguments, checks/pulls Docker images, runs a multiprocessing pool of Volatility commands, and invokes strings extraction — 'runner' reveals none of this
issues.suggestion: Rename to run_analysis() or orchestrate_scan() to communicate that this is the primary scan orchestration entry point, not a generic wrapper

issues.summary: getCommands() uses camelCase in a codebase where all other functions use snake_case
evidence: def getCommands(self, opsys) at line 124 is the only camelCase method in the file; all sibling methods (resolve_path, execute_command_volatility2, safe_print) use snake_case; multi_volatility3.py also uses getCommands(), propagating the outlier
issues.suggestion: Rename to get_commands() in both multi_volatility2.py and multi_volatility3.py and update all call sites in multivol.py

issues.summary: dump_dir parameter in execute_command_volatility2 holds a full file path, not a directory
evidence: def execute_command_volatility2(self, command, dump, dump_dir, ...) at line 34; line 43 has an inline comment 'dump_dir here is actually the full file path from main.py'; it is passed to resolve_path(os.path.abspath(dump_dir), host_path) and bound as a Docker volume mapping to /dumps/{dump}
issues.suggestion: Rename the parameter to dump_path or dump_file_path to match what it actually holds; update all call sites in multivol.py accordingly

issues.summary: 'hash' key in evidence API response holds a filename string, not a hash value
evidence: In list_evidences(), the evidence group dict includes '"hash": source_dump' (line 198) and '"hash": item' (line 232) where both source_dump and item are filename strings like 'memorydump.vmem' — a developer consuming this API field would expect a SHA-256 or similar hash value
issues.suggestion: Rename the field to source_filename or dump_filename in the API response; if an actual file hash is needed for integrity verification, populate it from get_file_hash() and keep the field name 'hash'

root_causes: Generic-verb naming (manage, runner, process) likely reflects incremental function growth — functions that started small accumulated responsibility without a rename to match
The camelCase getCommands outlier suggests the class was written by a different developer or at a different time than the rest of the codebase
The hash/filename mismatch in the API response may be a legacy field repurposed as a lookup key without updating the name
